title: Let them off. Then get on.
norm: Stand to the side of the door and let people get off the bus or train before you board.
wit: Two crowds trying to pass through one door is just physics, losing.
affects: elders and children in the crush
goesRight: Off first, on second, and the doorway clears in seconds - the train waits less and nobody gets crushed.

impact.cause: Boarding while others are still trying to get off
impact.mechanism: Two opposing crowds jam the doorway, so the doors stay open longer at every stop and the crush-risk rises
impact.effect: Slower journeys for everyone and a real fall-and-injury risk for elders and children
impact.publisher: Transit operations research (e.g. Transportation Research Board)
impact.supported_by: Simultaneous boarding and alighting increases door dwell time and passenger conflict at the doorway.
impact.caveat: Well-established operations principle; we show the mechanism rather than an Indian-network number.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: The priority seat is a promise.
norm: Keep the reserved seat free for those who need it - elders, pregnant women, and people with disabilities - and give yours up if asked.
wit: It costs you a two-minute stand. It saves someone a fall.
goesRight: A free priority seat lets the people who most need to sit travel safely, with dignity, and without asking.

impact.cause: Occupying a reserved seat when you don't need it
impact.mechanism: People with limited mobility must stand and are thrown off balance by braking and jolts in a moving vehicle
impact.effect: Avoidable falls and injuries, and the everyday indignity of being left standing
impact.source_title: Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 - accessible transport and reserved seating
impact.supported_by: Indian law provides for reserved seating and accessible public transport for persons with disabilities.
impact.caveat: Framed as the reason for the norm, not as a measured injury statistic.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: The queue is holding. So can you.
norm: Join the back of the line and wait your turn. Cutting in doesn't shorten the wait - it just moves it onto everyone behind you.
wit: A queue is a small miracle: strangers agreeing on an order. Don't be the one who breaks it.
affects: the person who was actually next
goesRight: When everyone holds the line, the wait is shorter and fairer than any shoving scrum could ever be.

impact.cause: Cutting in or jumping the queue instead of waiting your turn
impact.mechanism: A queue is a first-come, first-served ordering held up by a shared social norm; cutting the line breaks that norm and takes the place and time that belong to those who waited
impact.effect: The fair ordering collapses and everyone behind loses their rightful place and waits longer
impact.supported_by: Queue areas are places in which people queue (first-come, first-served) for goods or services.
impact.caveat: An openly-checkable statement of the first-come, first-served queue norm - the fairness a line depends on - not an India-specific statistic.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: In a crowd, don't push.
norm: In a dense crowd - a platform, a gate, a gathering - don't push forward. Pressure builds body against body until people can't breathe or even stand.
affects: children and elders in the crush
goesRight: A crowd that moves in slow, patient steps lets everyone through - and everyone home.

impact.cause: Pushing forward in a dense, tightly packed crowd
impact.mechanism: In a very dense crowd, pushing and leaning pass horizontal force from body to body, and that pressure squeezes people's chests so they cannot breathe
impact.effect: Compressive asphyxia - the cause of virtually all crowd-crush deaths - as people are crushed where they stand
impact.source_title: Behaviour and Mechanics of Crowd Crush Disasters
impact.supported_by: Horizontal forces sufficient to cause compressive asphyxia are more dynamic as people push off against each other to obtain breathing space.
impact.caveat: A crowd-physics source explaining how crush deaths happen, not an India-specific casualty count; no official Indian stampede figure could be verified from a citable source for this card.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.
